1: The epilogue returns to the imperial frame: Ahasuerus imposes tribute (mas) on the land and the islands of the sea (ʾiyyê hayyām). The imperial business continues; the Persian empire did not change its character because the Jews were preserved within it. The reference to tribute is not incidental — it establishes that Mordecai’s position exists within the ongoing structures of Persian power, not in a transformed political order. The book ends in the real world of empire, not in an eschatological resolution.
2: “All the acts of the king’s power (tōqep) and strength (gᵉḇûrāh), and the full account of the greatness of Mordecai (gᵉḏullat mŏrᵃḏᵃkay) to which the king advanced him (giḏḏᵉlô hammelek) — are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Media and Persia?” The official Persian chronicles that recorded Mordecai’s forgotten service in Esther 6:1-3 now record his greatness; the same archive that nearly failed him becomes the permanent register of his honor. The phrase “the book of the chronicles of the kings of Media and Persia” is the exact archive cited in Ezra 4:15 and Esther 6:2 — a historically verifiable imperial record.
3: “For Mordecai the Jew was second only to King Ahasuerus (mišneh lammelek ʾᵃḥašwērôš) and great among the Jews, beloved by the multitude of his people (rāṣûy lᵉrōḇ ʾeḥāyw), seeking the good of his people (dōrēš ṭôḇ lᵉʿammô) and speaking peace to all his descendants.” The phrase mišneh lammelek (second to the king) is the exact position held by Joseph (Genesis 41:40 — ʿal-kol-ʿammî yiššaq) and by Daniel (Daniel 5:29 — taltî bᵉmalkûtāʾ). The diaspora ideal — the faithful Jew who rises to the second rank in a pagan court and uses that position for the welfare of his people — reaches its most complete formulation here. Dōrēš ṭôḇ lᵉʿammô — seeking the good of his people — is the covenant leader’s vocation, last used in this sense for Israel’s ideal kings in the Psalms and Prophets.
